title: The Cover Up

jokes.text: Well, you know what? Joke's on you. 'cause I know morse code. Ha! Yeah. That's what we're doing. In our very limited free time And with our very limited budget, We went and got a nanny and then we went out And took a class on a very outmoded And very unnecessary form of communication just so We could talk about you in front of you.
jokes.setup: Dwight accuses Jim and Pam of talking about him in morse code by tapping
jokes.punchline: Pam's elaborate sarcastic response detailing the absurd effort this would require
jokes.explanation: Strong escalating sarcasm with good character voice. The specificity of 'very outmoded and very unnecessary form of communication' elevates it above basic sarcasm.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Yeah, Dwight, that's exactly what we're doing. We spent three hours last night learning morse code. Jim practiced on a telegraph machine he built in his garage. We've been coordinating our taps so perfectly that we can discuss your beet farm, your volunteer sheriff duties, and your weird obsession with bears all without making a sound. Yesterday we tapped out a 47-character message about your stapler. It took 20 minutes. Totally worth it.

jokes.text: It all started when dwight was tapping his foot Against the leg of his desk. When I asked him to stop, he said, 'I will when you lose the baby weight.'
jokes.setup: Explaining the morse code paranoia origin
jokes.punchline: Dwight's cruel baby weight comment as the real trigger
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight character moment - inappropriate and cruel in his typical way. The casual delivery makes the cruelty funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I was fine until Dwight said my baby looked like a baked potato.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Well, you were carrying a lot of water weight.

jokes.text: Very well. I must have imagined it. I apologize.
jokes.setup: Dwight's paranoid accusation being thoroughly debunked
jokes.punchline: His overly formal, immediate surrender
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Dwight's formal speech patterns and quick retreat when proven wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I demand to know who ate my lunch!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Dwight, your lunch is right there in the fridge. We all watched you put it in this morning.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Yeah, and you've been staring at it all day.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Oh. I see. Well then, I formally retract my accusation and offer my sincerest apologies to all parties involved. I shall submit my resignation effective immediately.

jokes.text: Jim. Are you clicking a detonator? - It's a pen. - Michael, come on.
jokes.setup: Michael's paranoid question about Jim clicking something
jokes.punchline: The mundane reality that it's just a pen
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective absurdist humor playing on Michael's tendency toward paranoid thinking.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Why do you keep clicking that?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: It's a pen.

jokes.text: They're going great. I, uh... We're just clicking on every level. Emotionally and sexually and...Orally
jokes.setup: Michael describing his relationship in professional terms
jokes.punchline: The awkward addition of 'orally' with the pause
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael oversharing with awkward sexual reference. The pause before 'orally' sells the discomfort.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Our relationship is like a business merger. We're combining our assets, our resources, our... our bodies. Orally.

jokes.text: * and I like it * Who enjoys the weekends?
jokes.setup: Michael singing to himself
jokes.punchline: The abrupt transition to asking about weekends
jokes.explanation: Mild character moment showing Michael's goofy happiness, but not particularly clever or memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: 🎵 I'm not superstitious, but I am a little stitious... 🎵

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: So, what did everybody do this weekend?

jokes.text: She has a pilates butt. But we need to find something to do this weekend Beside have sex. Did I say that? Yes, I did.
jokes.setup: Michael describing his girlfriend to employees
jokes.punchline: Self-aware acknowledgment of his oversharing
jokes.explanation: Good Michael moment - inappropriate oversharing followed by self-awareness that doesn't stop him.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: And that's why she won't return my calls. But you know what? I'm okay with it because I've already told you guys everything about her anyway.

jokes.text: Okay, you get out of here, big dog.
jokes.setup: Stanley wanting to leave the meeting
jokes.punchline: Michael's weird 'big dog' dismissal
jokes.explanation: Weird Michael-ism that's quotable but not particularly sophisticated humor.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: Can I go?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No. Sit down. I'm not done yet.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: But I have a lot to do.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Stanley, you know what? You're like a big dog and I'm gonna keep you here until you learn to sit.

jokes.text: Yeah, well, stanley doesn't help with anything.
jokes.setup: Explaining why Stanley can leave
jokes.punchline: Blunt assessment of Stanley's unhelpfulness
jokes.explanation: Simple observational humor about Stanley's work ethic, delivered with Michael's casual cruelty.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Stanley, you can leave whenever you want. You're not helping anyone here anyway.

jokes.text: Room enough for three.
jokes.setup: Suggesting heart-shaped jacuzzis for Michael's romantic weekend
jokes.punchline: The creepy implication of threesome
jokes.explanation: Perfect Todd Packer line - economical, inappropriate, and completely in character. The brevity makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Todd Packer
dialogue.line: Heart-shaped jacuzzis are perfect for romance.

dialogue.speaker: Michael Scott
dialogue.line: A heart-shaped jacuzzi? That's amazing!

dialogue.speaker: Todd Packer
dialogue.line: Yeah, and they come with a second jet setting for when you've got company.

dialogue.speaker: Michael Scott
dialogue.line: Wait, what do you mean 'company'?

dialogue.speaker: Todd Packer
dialogue.line: You know, a third person. For balance.

jokes.text: Because when your super hot girlfriend says, 'I wanna go to mount pocono,' You go to mount pocono and you do her. And we screwed. Whoops. Tmi.
jokes.setup: Explaining why he drove far for P.F. Chang's
jokes.punchline: The crude double entendre and fake 'whoops' about TMI
jokes.explanation: Typical Michael sexual oversharing with a weak pun. The 'whoops TMI' shows he knows it's inappropriate but doesn't care.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I drove all the way to the other side of town for P.F. Chang's because their lettuce wraps are... well, they're out of this world. You know what I'm saying?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I mean, I really know how to... work those wraps. Whoops, that came out wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: TMI, right? Too much information!

jokes.text: You know what, kelly? This is the real world. Not the real world: Scranton.
jokes.setup: Kelly suggesting Donna might be cheating
jokes.punchline: Michael's confused reality TV reference
jokes.explanation: Clever meta joke playing on both reality TV and the show's setting. Good wordplay with layered meaning.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. Is this like The Bachelor? Because if Donna's the bachelor, then... who's giving out the roses?

jokes.text: Some diner? It was the starlight diner. It's, it's in a life magazine spread About americana.
jokes.setup: Kelly calling it 'some diner' where Ryan took her
jokes.punchline: Ryan's pretentious defense of the diner's cultural significance
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ryan character moment - turning a simple diner into something culturally significant to defend his pretentious image.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: You took me to some diner.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: It's not just a diner, Kelly. It's a nostalgic throwback to mid-century American dining culture. The aesthetic alone is a commentary on post-war consumerism and the democratization of fine dining.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: It's a diner, Ryan.

jokes.text: I said it on the second date. - Mm. - That seems...Quick. Even for lesbians.
jokes.setup: Michael saying 'I love you' early in the relationship
jokes.punchline: Oscar's comment about lesbian relationship pace
jokes.explanation: Smart joke subverting expectations - using lesbian stereotype about relationship speed to highlight how fast Michael moved.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I love you.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: You've been dating for like three weeks. That's lesbian relationship pace.

jokes.text: Does she keep her phone locked around you, michael? Does she watch how much she drinks around you, michael? Does she leave the room when she takes phone calls? Does she keep perfume in her purse? Does she shower before sex? Does she shower after sex? Does she-- - yeah, she does all that.
jokes.setup: Kelly rapid-firing cheating warning signs
jokes.punchline: Michael's dejected admission that she does all of them
jokes.explanation: Great escalating structure with Kelly's machine-gun delivery building to Michael's defeated confirmation. Good comedic timing.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: If your man is texting other girls late at night, that's a red flag. If he's suddenly going to the gym, that's a red flag. If he buys new cologne, red flag. If he mentions an ex, red flag. If he looks at another woman for more than three seconds, red flag.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: So... Pam does all of those things.

jokes.text: Why didn't you just say that, pam? Michael, do not let your imagination Run out of control. Well, that's easy for you to say. You have a bad imagination. It's stupid.
jokes.setup: Pam using the word 'amok' and having to explain it
jokes.punchline: Michael's childish insult about Pam's imagination
jokes.explanation: Good Michael character moment showing his petty defensiveness when he doesn't understand something.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: The copier is running amok.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: What does that mean?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: It means out of control, like it's just going wild.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Well, your imagination is running amok. Actually, your imagination isn't even running. It's more like... walking. Slowly.

jokes.text: I live in a fantasy world. - You do? - Yes, jim, I do.
jokes.setup: Michael defending his imagination
jokes.punchline: His matter-of-fact admission that he lives in a fantasy world
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - Michael's rare moment of self-awareness delivered with his typical confidence.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm not insane, I just have a very active imagination. I can go anywhere, be anyone, do anything.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Sometimes I'm a secret agent. Sometimes I'm a astronaut. Sometimes I'm both.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Michael, that's just called having an imagination.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, Jim. You don't understand. I actually live in a fantasy world. I'm barely here right now.

jokes.text: But you didn't believe any of this was true Five minutes ago. That's what makes it so wrong.
jokes.setup: Jim pointing out Michael's rapid change of opinion
jokes.punchline: Michael's illogical reasoning that the speed makes it worse
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist logic from Michael - his reasoning makes no sense but is delivered with complete conviction.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You just completely changed your mind.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No I didn't.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yes, you did. Like thirty seconds ago you said the opposite.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Well, I have new information.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What new information? Nothing happened.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I thought about it more. And thinking is information.

jokes.text: $100 a day, plus expenses. I'll give you $50. Money's no object.
jokes.setup: Dwight quoting his PI rates
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately halving it while claiming money's no object
jokes.explanation: Perfect contradiction joke with great economy. Michael's cheapness vs. his claims is classic character contradiction.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: My rates are $500 a day, plus expenses.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's a little steep. How about $250 a day?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Michael, that's half my rate.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I know, but money's no object when it comes to solving crimes.

jokes.text: You might not like what I find. Okay. And you might not like how I find it.
jokes.setup: Standard PI warning about not liking results
jokes.punchline: Dwight's ominous addition about not liking his methods
jokes.explanation: Good Dwight character moment suggesting his investigation methods will be questionable/extreme.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: And you're not going to like my methods.

jokes.text: My doctor told me to cut out hot dogs. We all got problems.
jokes.setup: Jim explaining a serious printer fire issue
jokes.punchline: Stanley's dismissive comparison to his hot dog restriction
jokes.explanation: Perfect Stanley line - deadpan delivery equating serious business problems with personal inconvenience. Economical and character-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Stanley, there was a fire in the printer room. We need to evacuate immediately.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: A fire? That's nothing compared to the time they took away my hot dogs in the break room.

jokes.text: Two years ago, Andy blamed the warehouse for a late shipment That he forgot to process. We got yelled at pretty bad. Almost lost my job, And I was mad as hell at the time. But I said, 'darryl, just wait. he's a fool, there's gonna be an opportunity. Just be patient.'
jokes.setup: Darryl explaining past grievance with Andy
jokes.punchline: His patient plotting for revenge opportunity
jokes.explanation: Good character setup showing Darryl's long-term thinking and patience for revenge, though somewhat verbose.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Andy Bernard. That name still gets to me. You know why? Because he owes me money from a bet we made years ago. I've been waiting. Plotting. Every single day I come to work, I think about how I'm gonna get him back. And one day... one day the opportunity's gonna present itself. And when it does, I'm gonna be ready. I'm gonna walk up to him real slow, and I'm gonna say, 'Andy, remember me? Remember that fifty bucks?' And the look on his face is gonna be beautiful.

jokes.text: All cases are solved with logic. The only logical way to find out If donna is a cheater is to seduce her, Bring her to orgasm, then call michael And tell him the sad news.
jokes.setup: Dwight's logical approach to investigation
jokes.punchline: His completely illogical and inappropriate 'logical' solution
jokes.explanation: Excellent Dwight logic - using the word 'logical' for completely illogical and inappropriate behavior. Perfect character absurdism.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: As Assistant Regional Manager, it is my duty to investigate any suspicious activity in this office with the utmost logic and precision.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I have narrowed down the suspects to everyone. Therefore, I will interrogate them all using my fool-proof method: lie detector tests administered by me using this potato and a car battery.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: False. You're lying. I can tell by the way you're breathing oxygen.

jokes.text: It's never too early for ice cream, jim. But we didn't have any ice cream, So this is mayonnaise and black olives.
jokes.setup: Jim commenting on Michael eating ice cream early
jokes.punchline: The disgusting revelation of what he's actually eating
jokes.explanation: Brilliant gross-out visual gag. The casual delivery makes it funnier, and it perfectly shows Michael's emotional state through physical disgust.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Why are you eating ice cream at 9 AM?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: It's not ice cream.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What is it then?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: It's a mayonnaise and olive loaf sandwich that I've been keeping in the freezer.

jokes.text: It's comfort food, all right? God.
jokes.setup: Disgust at his mayonnaise and olives
jokes.punchline: Defending it as comfort food while clearly hating it
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to the mayonnaise gag, showing Michael's stubborn defense of obviously bad choices.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Look, it's comfort food, okay? My mother used to make this for me.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: It's... it's delicious. I love it.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Why are you all looking at me like that?

jokes.text: Otherwise this thing is gonna spiral out of amok.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to use the word 'amok'
jokes.punchline: His incorrect usage 'spiral out of amok'
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to the earlier 'amok' explanation, showing Michael still doesn't understand the phrase.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Things are starting to spiral out of amok.

jokes.text: You're right, I ruin everything. And I've known some wonderful women. Holly, carol, jan. Helene. - Helene? - My mother. - Oh. My mother, helene.
jokes.setup: Michael listing the great loves of his life
jokes.punchline: Including his mother in the romantic list
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection joke - the awkward pause before revelation makes it funnier. Classic Michael inappropriate inclusion.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: My great loves have been: my mother, my work, and my ability to connect with people.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Your mother?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Yes, she was a great love of my life.

jokes.text: Ah! Ah... - You okay? - Yeah, I'm good. Hey, you know an exercise for two people That uses the whole body? Yeah, I think I know what you're talking about. Tractor pulling.
jokes.setup: Dwight's suggestive question about two-person exercise
jokes.punchline: His completely unsexy answer of tractor pulling
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection - sets up sexual expectation then delivers absurdly unsexy Dwight answer. Perfect character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: What's a good two-person exercise?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Tractor pulling.

jokes.text: One thing you need to know about me, I don't quit until something tears or pops.
jokes.setup: Dwight trying to be seductive/impressive
jokes.punchline: His disturbing persistence philosophy
jokes.explanation: Great Dwight line - trying to be impressive but making it sound violent and disturbing. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I am like a hunter. Once I set my sights on something, I will pursue it relentlessly. I don't give up. I don't get tired. I am a machine.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Some might call it harassment. I call it persistence. And that is what separates the winners from the losers.

jokes.text: Tomorrow's fertilizer, am I right?
jokes.setup: Making conversation with other gym member
jokes.punchline: His morbid small talk reference
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny Dwight moment - casual reference to death as fertilizer as gym small talk.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: You know, it's funny. My grandfather died on a treadmill. He was 103.

dialogue.speaker: Gym Member
dialogue.line: That's terrible.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Not really. He was a very active man.

jokes.text: We both know why I'm here: To see...You...Naked... While...I'm...Naked.
jokes.setup: Dwight's final seduction attempt
jokes.punchline: His incredibly awkward and creepy delivery
jokes.explanation: Peak cringe Dwight moment - the halting delivery makes his already inappropriate approach even more uncomfortable.

jokes.text: Who eats eight protein bars? People who don't trust egg whites.
jokes.setup: Michael questioning Dwight's expense receipts
jokes.punchline: Dwight's paranoid logic about egg whites
jokes.explanation: Great Dwight paranoid logic - finding conspiracy in basic food products. Good absurdist reasoning.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Dwight, these expense receipts don't make any sense. You're claiming egg whites as a business expense?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: That's right. I need to maintain peak physical condition to be an effective regional manager. Egg whites are a legitimate business expense.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You're not the regional manager, Dwight.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Not yet. But when I am, I'll already be in peak condition. It's preventative maintenance.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's not how expenses work.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: You're thinking about this all wrong. Every egg white I consume is an egg white that my competitors aren't consuming. It's basic business strategy.

jokes.text: What she actually said was, 'what is with him? He is crazy. I'm coming over there to talk to him.' And this was after I have no other recourse But to tell her and gym security That you had sent me there to see if she was cheating. Also, I joined the gym. You'll be billed monthly.
jokes.setup: Dwight reporting back on his investigation
jokes.punchline: The casual addition of billing Michael for the gym membership
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight opportunism - turning his failure into a billing opportunity. Good escalating reveal structure.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I've completed my investigation. Michael has been using the company gym membership for personal use. I'm recommending we bill him for it.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Wait, what? Dwight, that's my gym membership!

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Exactly. $50 a month, retroactive six months. That's $300.

jokes.text: You work at an adult arcade. You could have any man you want.
jokes.setup: Michael explaining why he's insecure about Donna
jokes.punchline: His view of an adult arcade as a place of high romantic opportunity
jokes.explanation: Good Michael moment showing his skewed perspective on what makes someone desirable/available.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm insecure about Donna because she's successful, she's confident, she's beautiful. And I'm... I'm me. So I need to find a place where I can be the big man on campus. And that place is the adult arcade.

jokes.text: Well, you are boner-ific.
jokes.setup: Donna accepting Michael despite his behavior
jokes.punchline: Her crude but affectionate made-up word
jokes.explanation: Mildly clever portmanteau that's more crude than funny, but economical and memorable.

jokes.text: Poop my pants.
jokes.setup: Donna asking what he'd do if she suggested a trip
jokes.punchline: Michael's crude expression of excitement
jokes.explanation: Crude Michael moment that relies on toilet humor without much craft. Economical but not clever.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I would be so excited I'd probably jizz in my pants.

jokes.text: I don't have a plan exactly. More of a loose structure. Gives me freedom to improvise. It's like jazz.
jokes.setup: Andy explaining his investigation approach
jokes.punchline: His pretentious jazz comparison for having no plan
jokes.explanation: Good Andy character moment - using pretentious artistic references to mask his lack of preparation.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: My investigation approach? I like to think of it as jazz. You know, just improvise, see where it takes you, no real plan.

jokes.text: How about, andy is a problem and we must eliminate him?
jokes.setup: Andy mishearing Gabe's conversation
jokes.punchline: His matter-of-fact statement about his own elimination
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny moment with Andy casually discussing his own corporate elimination. Good deadpan delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: So Gabe's finally getting rid of me. Well, I guess that's what I get for not being a suck-up.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I mean, I've always known I was expendable. We all are, really.

jokes.text: Claire's? Zales? Ricky's? Earrings earrings earrings? Fancy girl? Platinum cat? Where?
jokes.setup: Pam asking where Donna got her earrings
jokes.punchline: The increasingly ridiculous made-up store names
jokes.explanation: Great escalating absurdism with increasingly silly store names. 'Earrings earrings earrings?' and 'Platinum cat?' are particularly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Where did you get those earrings? They're so cute.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Oh, I got them at this little boutique called Ear Candy Palace.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I've never heard of that store.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's pretty obscure. They also have a sister store called Lobe Lounge Extravaganza.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: That's... a lot of words.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: And if you're lucky, you can catch their pop-up at the Dangly Things Emporium of Wonder.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I don't think any of those places are real.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Well, they are now, in your heart.

jokes.text: So there I am, minding my own business, And darnell offers me three bucks. All I gotta do is walk by andy And go like this. Darnell's a chump. I would have done it for anything. I've done a lot more for a lot less.
jokes.setup: Creed explaining his part in the prank
jokes.punchline: His admission of doing worse things for less money
jokes.explanation: Perfect Creed moment - casual admission of questionable past activities. The vague 'I've done a lot more for a lot less' is delightfully ominous.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: I've done a lot worse things than that for a lot less money.

jokes.text: It's cece. She's never gonna do anything wrong. - Isn't that something? - Wow.
jokes.setup: Jim showing photos to Michael
jokes.punchline: Michael's awkward attempt at appropriate baby talk
jokes.explanation: Mild character moment showing Michael's discomfort with appropriate baby conversation. Not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Look at these photos of the baby.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Oh my God, he's so cute! Hey little guy, who's a precious little dumpling? You are! Yes you are! You're gonna grow up to be strong and handsome like your daddy, Jim. Or maybe you'll be successful like me. Either way, you're gonna make six figures and drive a sensible sedan.

jokes.text: Yes. Yes, I desperately want to speak with you About my sex life with jim.
jokes.setup: Michael assuming Pam wants to discuss marital problems
jokes.punchline: Pam's sarcastic agreement to discussing her sex life
jokes.explanation: Solid sarcastic response from Pam, shutting down Michael's inappropriate assumptions about her marriage.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Pam, I know what this is about. You want to talk about your marital problems.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Yes, Michael. That's exactly what I want to do. I want to discuss my sex life with my boss in the middle of the office.

jokes.text: Who the hell is this? - I don't know who he is. - God! Lowest of the low. That guy. Just a notch above toby.
jokes.setup: Michael seeing Donna's husband in photos
jokes.punchline: Rating the husband just above Toby in his hierarchy of hatred
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Michael's Toby hatred, using it as the baseline for measuring terrible people. Classic Michael ranking system.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I mean, look at this guy. He's... he's not even that bad. I'd say he's like a 6 out of 10 in terms of people I hate.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Which puts him just above Toby. Toby's a solid 10.

jokes.text: That's good for five bucks at dunkin' donuts. Any dunkin' donuts.
jokes.setup: Gabe rewarding Jim for bringing printer problems to attention
jokes.punchline: The pathetically small reward with unnecessary specification
jokes.explanation: Good corporate humor - the tiny reward and unnecessary clarification about 'any' Dunkin' Donuts shows corporate cheapness.

dialogue.speaker: Gabe
dialogue.line: Jim, I'm going to give you a gift card.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Oh, wow, thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Gabe
dialogue.line: It's a $5 gift card to Applebee's. It's only redeemable on a Tuesday.

jokes.text: Spice girls are opening for weird al. Front row.
jokes.setup: Michael suggesting a concert date
jokes.punchline: The backwards billing of this imaginary concert
jokes.explanation: Funny absurdist concert lineup with the Spice Girls opening for Weird Al. Good Michael fantasy moment.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: We could go see a concert. I'll pay for everything.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: We'll go see... I don't know, The Police reunion tour or something.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: And then afterwards, they bill it as 'An evening with the woman from Scranton.'

jokes.text: I don't wanna prank anymore. Things get real. It's not funny. I'm just gonna be good, stay in my room, Go to church, Try to do one nice thing per day. I do not wanna prank anymore.
jokes.setup: Andy celebrating their successful printer fire prank
jokes.punchline: Darryl's frightened moral awakening from the escalation
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Darryl's immediate regret when pranks become dangerous. The repetition emphasizes his fear.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: We did it! The printer fire prank was a complete success!

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa. A fire? An actual fire? That's not a prank, that's a crime. That's arson!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Come on, it was just a small fire. Nobody got hurt.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Just a small fire?! There is no such thing as a small fire in a workplace! What's next, are we gonna poison the water cooler? Fake a lockdown? I can't be part of this. I have a daughter!

jokes.text: I'm the mistress?
jokes.setup: Donna revealing she's married and Michael is the other man
jokes.punchline: Michael's shocked realization of his role reversal
jokes.explanation: Excellent punchline to the episode's main plot. Perfect economy, great role reversal, and Michael's delivery would be perfectly stunned. Strong episode climax.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. You're married? So I'm the... I'm the other man?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I've never been the other man before. I don't like it. I don't like how it feels.

tmdb_overview: The press finds out that Sabre’s printers catch on fire. Jo shows up to the office in hopes of finding out who the whistleblower is. Everyone suspects Andy, who adamantly denies he leaked the information.
insight: Season 6 finale leans hard on cringe over laugh density, landing below average.
